definition: Fixed Deposit — you lock money in a bank for a fixed period and earn guaranteed interest.
A savings product where you deposit a lump sum for a fixed tenure at a guaranteed interest rate.
Tax Deducted at Source — the bank automatically deducts 10% tax on FD interest above ₹40,000/year before paying you.
Deposit Insurance and Credit Guarantee Corporation — a government body that insures your bank deposits up to ₹5 lakh per bank.
The date when your FD period ends and the bank returns your principal + interest.
The original amount you deposit in the FD, before any interest is added.
The duration for which you lock your money in an FD — can range from 7 days to 10 years.
Interest calculated on both the principal and previously earned interest. Most Indian bank FDs compound quarterly.
Interest calculated only on the principal amount. Formula: P × R × T / 100.
Breaking your FD before the maturity date. Banks charge a 0.5–1% penalty on the interest rate.
A self-declaration form submitted to the bank to avoid TDS deduction if your total income is below the taxable limit.
Same as Form 15G but for senior citizens (age 60+).
Non-Banking Financial Company — offers FDs at higher rates than banks but without DICGC insurance protection.
An FD where interest is not paid periodically but added to the principal and paid at maturity.
An FD where interest is paid out monthly, quarterly, or annually instead of at maturity.
Special FD for people aged 60+ with an extra 0.25–0.5% interest rate over regular rates.
An FD with a 5-year lock-in that qualifies for tax deduction up to ₹1.5 lakh under Section 80C.
Section 80C of the Income Tax Act — allows deduction up to ₹1.5 lakh for tax-saving FDs, PPF, ELSS, etc.
A facility where excess money in your savings account is automatically transferred to an FD to earn higher interest.
A flexible FD linked to your savings account — you can withdraw partially without breaking the entire FD.
